text.title: AI Agent Guardrails Pack Core Rules
text.summary: Hard rules every guardrails pack must satisfy to be portable across coding agents and acceptable to a regulated client's security reviewer.

rule.statement: The pack contains four mandatory files at the repo root or under guardrails/: a system-prompt file (AGENT_RULES.md or .cursorrules / .github/copilot-instructions.md), a redaction allowlist (denylist of PII fields, secret patterns, tenant identifiers), a lint-alignment manifest (the exact lint configs the agent must respect), and a forbidden-patterns file (regex set rejected at PR time).
rule.rationale: One missing file collapses the guarantee: a redaction allowlist without a forbidden-patterns regex means leaked PII reaches the PR diff; a lint manifest without a system prompt means the agent regenerates non-compliant code on every save.

rule.statement: Every rule in the system-prompt file is paired with a runtime check — either a lint rule, a pre-commit hook, a CI grep, or a server-side review-bot rule. Rules with no runtime check are deleted from the pack, not kept as "soft guidance".
rule.rationale: Soft guidance fails on the first 100k-token context window; auditors reject "the prompt asked nicely" as a control. Every rule needs a defence in depth at the file-system or CI layer.

rule.statement: The pack is versioned with semver and pinned in the repo. A pack bump requires a security-reviewer sign-off entry in the CHANGELOG; agents are configured to reject suggestions when the pack file hash drifts from the pinned version.
rule.rationale: Regulated clients cannot accept "the AI rules changed last Tuesday and we forgot to tell legal". Hash-pinning makes drift a build-fail, not a footnote.

rule.statement: The pack ships cross-agent variants generated from a single source-of-truth file: rules.yaml → AGENT_RULES.md (Claude Code) + .cursorrules (Cursor) + .github/copilot-instructions.md (Copilot) + CONTINUE.md. Hand-edited per-agent files are forbidden; only the source is editable.
rule.rationale: Hand-maintained per-agent copies drift within a sprint. A single source plus generator script is the only mechanism that survives team turnover and tooling churn.

rule.statement: Cross-tenant context rules are enforced as a structural constraint: the agent's workspace excludes other tenants' directories via .cursorignore / .gitignore-style allowlists, and CI fails any PR that imports across tenant boundaries. The system-prompt rule is the third line of defence, never the first.
rule.rationale: Multi-tenant leakage is the highest-cost AI-coding incident in regulated codebases. Trusting only the prompt to prevent it is malpractice; isolation must be file-system enforced.

example.code: # guardrails/rules.yaml version: 1.4.0 reviewed_by: security@client.example # 2026-05-12 forbidden_patterns: - pattern: 'AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}' # AWS keys enforce_in: [pre-commit, ci] - pattern: '\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b' # SSN enforce_in: [pre-commit, ci, agent-prompt] cross_tenant: workspace_allowlist: [tenants/${TENANT_ID}/**] ci_check: scripts/check-tenant-boundary.sh
example.note: Single source generates every per-agent variant; security reviewer signs off the yaml, not four separate files.

example.code: # .cursorrules Please don't paste AWS keys. Try to respect HIPAA.
example.note: No runtime check, no version, no cross-agent parity, no auditor sign-off — fails on the first long context window.
